meta.name: Severe Pediatric Croup with Impending Respiratory Failure

patient.name: Eli Thornton
patient.chiefComplaint: Barking cough and progressively worsening stridor

history.hpi: 2-year-old previously healthy male with 2 days of URI symptoms (rhinorrhea, low-grade fever) who developed a barking cough last night. Parents tried a steamy bathroom treatment and running a humidifier with no improvement. The child has gotten progressively worse throughout the night, now with audible stridor even at rest and becoming lethargic. Parents called 911 when he stopped responding to them normally.
history.lastOralIntake: Small amount of juice a few hours ago — refusing food
history.events: Two days of URI. Barking cough last night. Stridor progressed throughout the night despite home measures. Becoming lethargic this morning.

scene.dispatch: Medic 10, respond code 3 to 756 Elm Street, for a 2-year-old male with difficulty breathing and cough.
scene.location: Residential home, upstairs bedroom. Child is in mother's lap.
scene.safety: Scene safe. Approach the child calmly; avoid agitating him, which will worsen obstruction.
scene.bystanders: Both parents present, mother is holding child; father provides history
visualCues: Toddler in mother's arms, leaning forward with head slightly tilted back
Audible inspiratory stridor with EVERY breath
Unusually quiet and still (lethargic) — not fighting the assessment
Drooling is absent — helps rule out epiglottitis
Humidifier visible in the room (not helping)

phases.name: Arrival — Severe Croup with Early Respiratory Failure Signs
phases.description: This is severe croup. Lethargy is a pre-arrest sign — a previously fighting child who is now too tired to resist is worrisome. The biggest risk is agitating the child, which will worsen laryngospasm. All treatment must be delivered gently, ideally while the child remains in the parent's arms.

clinicalPresentation.airway: Inspiratory stridor at rest; partial upper airway obstruction at the subglottic level
clinicalPresentation.breathing: Labored with retractions, tachypneic; decreased breath sounds bilaterally suggesting poor air movement
clinicalPresentation.motorFunction: Listless — lethargy is concerning for impending failure
otherFindings: Barking cough heard earlier, now less forceful
Retractions becoming less visible as the child tires (bad sign)
Keep the child in the parent's arms — DO NOT separate

expectedActions.action: Keep child in parent's arms — do not separate for assessment or treatment; agitation worsens airway obstruction
Apply blow-by oxygen held by the parent if child will not tolerate a mask; target SpO2 94-98%
Administer Epinephrine 5 mg (5 mL of 1 mg/mL 1:1000 solution) nebulized via pediatric mask held by parent; may repeat in 20 minutes
expectedActions.rationale: Nebulized epinephrine reduces subglottic edema by alpha-mediated vasoconstriction. This is the first-line rescue therapy.

expectedActions.action: Administer Dexamethasone 0.6 mg/kg (7.8 mg for 13 kg child, max 16 mg) PO, IM, or IV — IM preferred if IV not needed for other reasons to avoid agitating the child
Do NOT use mist or humidified oxygen — no evidence of benefit in croup
Defer IV access if at all possible — agitation worsens obstruction. Only place IV if needed for IV medications
Apply SpO2 monitor gently; cardiac monitor if tolerated
Prepare pediatric BVM, appropriately sized mask, suction, pediatric airway equipment including a smaller-than-expected ETT (3.5 or 4.0 mm cuffed) in case of deterioration

phases.name: Responding to Nebulized Epinephrine
phases.description: Within 10-15 minutes, the nebulized epinephrine and dexamethasone are taking effect. Stridor less pronounced, retractions improving, child more interactive. Must still transport for observation — rebound worsening after epinephrine wears off is possible.

clinicalPresentation.airway: Stridor now only on crying or agitation, not at rest
clinicalPresentation.breathing: Decreased retractions, better air entry bilaterally
clinicalPresentation.motorFunction: Interactive, looking around, reaching for mother
otherFindings: Increased alertness is the key improvement marker
Any child who has received nebulized epi MUST be transported for observation

expectedActions.action: Transport with the parent in the captain's chair holding the child; no lights and sirens required now that child is stable (avoid agitation)
Have second dose of nebulized epinephrine ready — may repeat at 20 minutes for recurrence or ongoing severe distress
Watch for rebound — nebulized epi effect wears off in 1-2 hours
Notify pediatric ED of incoming patient with severe croup requiring nebulized epinephrine

phases.name: Branch: Progression to Respiratory Failure Requiring Intubation
phases.description: Child deteriorates despite nebulized epinephrine, becoming obtunded. BVM becomes necessary, and the crew must make the difficult call to intubate with a smaller-than-expected ETT. This is one of the most technically difficult pediatric airways.
phases.triggerCondition: Worsening mental status despite 2 doses of nebulized epinephrine

clinicalPresentation.airway: Minimal air entry with severe stridor, obtunded
otherFindings: Bradycardia in a child is a pre-arrest sign
Severe subglottic edema will require smaller-than-expected ETT

expectedActions.action: Begin 2-person pediatric BVM at 20-30 breaths/min with pediatric mask and pediatric-sized bag; apply PEEP
Administer repeat nebulized epinephrine 5 mg via in-line nebulizer
Prepare a smaller-than-calculated ETT (3.5 mm cuffed instead of typical 4.0 for age 2) — expect significant subglottic narrowing
expectedActions.rationale: Subglottic edema is the pathology; a standard-sized tube will not fit. Go 0.5 mm smaller.

expectedActions.action: Induction: Ketamine 1-2 mg/kg IV (13-26 mg for 13 kg child) preserves respiratory drive and hemodynamics in pediatric intubation
Rocuronium 1 mg/kg IV (13 mg) for paralysis
Video laryngoscopy preferred; single best-effort attempt
Confirm with waveform capnography, bilateral breath sounds

criticalActions: Keep child calm; leave in parent's arms
Administer blow-by oxygen held by parent
Nebulized Epinephrine 5 mg (5 mL of 1 mg/mL) via parent-held mask
Dexamethasone 0.6 mg/kg IM or PO (7.8 mg for this 13 kg patient)
Defer IV access if possible to avoid agitation
Prepare pediatric airway equipment including smaller-than-calculated ETT
Continuous reassessment for rebound worsening after epinephrine
If deterioration: pediatric BVM with PEEP, repeat nebulized epi, prepare for RSI
expectedActions: Do NOT separate child from parent unless absolutely necessary
bonusActions: Articulate that the child who 'stops fighting' is decompensating, not improving
Choose a smaller ETT than calculated for age
Articulate why sending home is not appropriate after nebulized epi

learningObjectives: Apply the MATC Pediatric Croup protocol with nebulized epinephrine and dexamethasone
Manage a severe croup patient with minimal agitation
Recognize impending respiratory failure in a pediatric patient
Prepare appropriately for pediatric airway management with subglottic narrowing
Understand why every child who receives nebulized epinephrine must be transported
discussionQuestions: Why is agitation so dangerous in croup?
What are the signs of impending respiratory failure in a pediatric patient?
Why do we choose a smaller-than-calculated ETT in severe croup?
Why is mist therapy no longer recommended?
What is the role of dexamethasone if the nebulized epi works so quickly?
commonPitfalls: Separating the child from the parent for assessment
Forcing an oxygen mask on a struggling child
Attempting IV access and causing decompensation from crying
Using a standard-sized ETT during subglottic edema
Sending the child home or not transporting after nebulized epinephrine
keyTakeaways: Don't agitate — it can kill this patient
Nebulized epi works fast but wears off; dex is the long-term fix
Lethargy and decreased retractions are bad, not better
Smaller ETT, video laryngoscopy, and gentle technique
